1:
"Then we turned, and went up the way to Bashan: and Og the king of Bashan came out against us, he and all his people, to battle at Edrei." The witnesses note Israel turning up to Bashan, and Og its king coming out with all his people to battle. The witnesses note that Gill notes Bashan was a fine country for pasturage and famous for its oaks, the same called Batanea. The witnesses note that Henry marks another brave country delivered into the hand of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turned up to Bashan — the rich country of oaks; the Og came out — the king with all his people; the to battle at Edrei — the aggressor marching out; the another country delivered — the victories coming swiftly.

The witnesses agree the second conquest follows hard upon the first: Israel turned up to Bashan, and Og, like Sihon before him, marched out to battle, walking into the same judgment; the bold aggression of yet another Amorite king — coming against the people God was leading — set the stage for a fresh display of the LORD's power, "another brave country delivered into their hand," the victories now coming swiftly as God went before his people.

So the witnesses make the verse Og comes out: The witnesses note the turned up to Bashan: the rich country of oaks. The witnesses note the Og came out: the king with all his people. The witnesses note the to battle at Edrei: the aggressor marching out. The witnesses note the another country delivered: the victories coming swiftly. The expositors observe the turned up to Bashan: the rich country of oaks. The expositors observe the Og came out: the king with all his people. The expositors observe the to battle at Edrei: the aggressor marching out. The expositors observe the another country delivered: the victories coming swiftly. The believer is taught to mark the turned up to Bashan, the Og came out, the to battle at Edrei, the another country delivered, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Israel turned up to Bashan and Og came out to battle — another aggressor walking into judgment, another country to be delivered into Israel's hand.
2:
"And the LORD said unto me, Fear him not: for I will deliver him, and all his people, and his land, into thy hand; and thou shalt do unto him as thou didst unto Sihon king of the Amorites..." The witnesses note God bidding Moses not to fear Og, promising to deliver him as he had Sihon. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was spoken when Og was marching with all his forces against Israel. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Bashan was named from its soft, level soil. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the fear him not — the command against dread; the I will deliver him — the promise of victory; the as thou didst unto Sihon — the same triumph pledged; the armed by his word — dread turned to certainty of victory.

The witnesses agree the word "fear him not" was the ground of Israel's courage against a giant king: though Og was mighty and came with all his force, God forbade fear and pledged the same victory he had given over Sihon; the command not to fear, joined to the promise of deliverance, teaches that the people of God are armed against the greatest terror by his word, the assurance "I will deliver him into thy hand" turning the dread of a giant into the certainty of triumph.

So the witnesses make the verse fear him not: The witnesses note the fear him not: the command against dread. The witnesses note the I will deliver him: the promise of victory. The witnesses note the as thou didst unto Sihon: the same triumph pledged. The witnesses note the armed by his word: dread turned to certainty of victory. The expositors observe the fear him not: the command against dread. The expositors observe the I will deliver him: the promise of victory. The expositors observe the as thou didst unto Sihon: the same triumph pledged. The expositors observe the armed by his word: dread turned to certainty of victory. The believer is taught to mark the fear him not, the I will deliver him, the as thou didst unto Sihon, the armed by his word, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that God bade Moses not fear Og but promised to deliver him as Sihon — the word "fear him not" arming Israel against a giant, dread turned to the certainty of triumph by God's promise.
3:
"So the LORD our God delivered into our hands Og also the king of Bashan, and all his people: and we smote him until none was left to him remaining." The witnesses note God delivering Og and all his people, none left remaining. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD delivered Og king of Bashan, as well as Sihon king of Heshbon. The witnesses note that none of his people was left remaining. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD delivered — og into their hands; the and all his people — the host overthrown; the none remaining — the overthrow complete; the the promise made good — i will deliver him fulfilled; the God's hand — no might of man, not even a giant's, standing against him.

The witnesses agree the victory over Og, like that over Sihon, is ascribed wholly to God: "the LORD our God delivered" him, the giant king and all his host overthrown till none remained; the complete overthrow made good the promise "I will deliver him," and proved again that no might of man — not even a giant's — can stand against the God who fights for his people, the second triumph confirming the first as the work of his hand.

So the witnesses make the verse Og delivered: The witnesses note the the LORD delivered: Og into their hands. The witnesses note the and all his people: the host overthrown. The witnesses note the none remaining: the overthrow complete. The witnesses note the the promise made good: I will deliver him fulfilled. The witnesses note the God's hand: no might of man, not even a giant's, standing against him. The expositors observe the the LORD delivered: Og into their hands. The expositors observe the and all his people: the host overthrown. The expositors observe the none remaining: the overthrow complete. The expositors observe the the promise made good: I will deliver him fulfilled. The expositors observe the God's hand: no might of man, not even a giant's, standing against him. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD delivered, the and all his people, the none remaining, the the promise made good, the God's hand, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that God delivered Og and all his people, none remaining — the giant king overthrown, the promise made good, no might of man standing against the God who fights for his people.
4:
"And we took all his cities at that time, there was not a city which we took not from them, threescore cities, all the region of Argob, the kingdom of Og in Bashan." The witnesses note Israel taking all Og's cities — threescore in the region of Argob. The witnesses note that Gill notes they took not only Edrei and Ashteroth his capital but all the rest in his kingdom. The witnesses note that Clarke notes "the region of Argob" is literally the "cord" or measured tract of Argob. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all his cities — not one untaken; the threescore cities — the whole kingdom; the the region of Argob — the measured tract of Bashan; the the full possession — god giving the land wholly.

The witnesses agree the completeness of the conquest — not one city left untaken, threescore in all — magnifies the thoroughness of God's gift: the whole kingdom of Og, a measured region of sixty cities, fell into Israel's hand; the sweeping victory, leaving no stronghold, shows that when God gives a land he gives it wholly, the people entering into the full possession he had promised, every city of the giant's realm delivered up.

So the witnesses make the verse all his cities taken: The witnesses note the all his cities: not one untaken. The witnesses note the threescore cities: the whole kingdom. The witnesses note the the region of Argob: the measured tract of Bashan. The witnesses note the the full possession: God giving the land wholly. The expositors observe the all his cities: not one untaken. The expositors observe the threescore cities: the whole kingdom. The expositors observe the the region of Argob: the measured tract of Bashan. The expositors observe the the full possession: God giving the land wholly. The believer is taught to mark the all his cities, the threescore cities, the the region of Argob, the the full possession, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Israel took all Og's threescore cities in Argob — the whole kingdom delivered up, God giving the land wholly, the people entering full possession.
5:
"All these cities were fenced with high walls, gates, and bars; beside unwalled towns a great many." The witnesses note the cities strongly fenced with high walls, gates, and bars — yet all fell. The witnesses note that Gill notes that though the cities were fenced with high walls, gates, and bars, it hindered not their falling into Israel's hands. The witnesses note that this might encourage them against the fears raised by the spies about the cities of Canaan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the high walls, gates, bars — the cities strongly fenced; the yet they fell — the fortifications no hindrance; the against the spies' fears — rebuking the old terror of walls; the no defence stands — against the God who gives victory.

The witnesses agree the falling of such strong cities was a designed encouragement against the old terror: the spies had once frightened Israel with the walled cities of Canaan, yet here the high-walled, barred cities of Bashan fell without hindrance; the lesson, pressed for the people's faith, is that no fortification can stand against the God who gives the victory, the strongest defences of men availing nothing — a rebuke to the unbelief that feared walls, and a ground of courage for the conquest ahead.

So the witnesses make the verse fenced cities fall: The witnesses note the high walls, gates, bars: the cities strongly fenced. The witnesses note the yet they fell: the fortifications no hindrance. The witnesses note the against the spies' fears: rebuking the old terror of walls. The witnesses note the no defence stands: against the God who gives victory. The expositors observe the high walls, gates, bars: the cities strongly fenced. The expositors observe the yet they fell: the fortifications no hindrance. The expositors observe the against the spies' fears: rebuking the old terror of walls. The expositors observe the no defence stands: against the God who gives victory. The believer is taught to mark the high walls, gates, bars, the yet they fell, the against the spies' fears, the no defence stands, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that the high-walled, barred cities of Bashan fell without hindrance — a rebuke to the unbelief that feared Canaan's walls, no fortification standing against the God who gives the victory.
6:
"And we utterly destroyed them, as we did unto Sihon king of Heshbon, utterly destroying the men, women, and children, of every city." The witnesses note the utter destruction of Bashan's people, as with Sihon — the cherem of divine judgment. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was the inhabitants, not the cities, that were utterly destroyed. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes "utterly destroyed" means devoted, made cherem, as before (Deut. 2:34). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the utterly destroyed them — the people devoted as cherem; the as unto Sihon — the same judgment repeated; the of every city — none spared from the sentence; the divine judgment — the Amorite's iniquity answered (Gen. 15:16).

The witnesses agree the destruction of Bashan, like that of Sihon, was the cherem of God's judgment, not human cruelty: the people of every city were devoted to destruction by divine sentence, the Amorite's long-ripened iniquity now answered; the repetition of the cherem upon a second kingdom shows that the same righteous judgment fell on all the doomed peoples alike, God using Israel as the instrument of his wrath upon nations ripe for it (cf. Gen. 15:16).

So the witnesses make the verse utterly destroyed: The witnesses note the utterly destroyed them: the people devoted as cherem. The witnesses note the as unto Sihon: the same judgment repeated. The witnesses note the of every city: none spared from the sentence. The witnesses note the divine judgment: the Amorite's iniquity answered (Gen. 15:16). The expositors observe the utterly destroyed them: the people devoted as cherem. The expositors observe the as unto Sihon: the same judgment repeated. The expositors observe the of every city: none spared from the sentence. The expositors observe the divine judgment: the Amorite's iniquity answered (Gen. 15:16). The believer is taught to mark the utterly destroyed them, the as unto Sihon, the of every city, the divine judgment, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Israel utterly destroyed Bashan's people as Sihon's — the cherem of God's judgment, the same righteous sentence on a second doomed kingdom (Gen. 15:16).
7:
"But all the cattle, and the spoil of the cities, we took for a prey to ourselves." The witnesses note the cattle and spoil of Bashan taken by Israel, while the people were destroyed. The witnesses note that Gill notes the oxen, sheep, camels, asses, gold, silver, household furniture, stores of corn, and all their substance were taken. The witnesses note that these were preserved as a prey for Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the cattle — spared from destruction; the the spoil of the cities — their goods and substance; the a prey to ourselves — the reward of victory; the measured justice — the guilty destroyed, the goods given.

The witnesses agree the sparing of the spoil marks again the measured justice of the cherem: the guilty inhabitants fell to the sword, but their goods were given to Israel as the reward and provision of victory; the same distinction kept in Sihon's case is kept here — the lives forfeit to judgment, the wealth granted to the conquerors — God both executing his sentence on the Amorite and enriching his people out of the same conquest.

So the witnesses make the verse the spoil taken: The witnesses note the all the cattle: spared from destruction. The witnesses note the the spoil of the cities: their goods and substance. The witnesses note the a prey to ourselves: the reward of victory. The witnesses note the measured justice: the guilty destroyed, the goods given. The expositors observe the all the cattle: spared from destruction. The expositors observe the the spoil of the cities: their goods and substance. The expositors observe the a prey to ourselves: the reward of victory. The expositors observe the measured justice: the guilty destroyed, the goods given. The believer is taught to mark the all the cattle, the the spoil of the cities, the a prey to ourselves, the measured justice, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Israel took the cattle and spoil of Bashan for themselves — the measured justice of the cherem, the guilty destroyed yet the wealth granted, God enriching his people out of the conquest.
8:
"And we took at that time out of the hand of the two kings of the Amorites the land that was on this side Jordan, from the river of Arnon unto mount Hermon;" The witnesses note the whole land of the two Amorite kings taken, from Arnon to Hermon. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was taken out of the hands of the two kings, Sihon of Heshbon and Og of Bashan. The witnesses note that Wesley notes "on this side Jordan" was so when Moses wrote, but called "beyond Jordan" after Israel crossed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the two Amorite kings — sihon and Og; the from Arnon to Hermon — the whole east-Jordan land; the on this side Jordan — as Moses wrote it then; the the first inheritance — secured before the crossing.

The witnesses agree the verse gathers up the whole east-Jordan conquest as the fruit of God's two victories: from Arnon in the south to Hermon in the north, the entire territory of both Amorite kings was now Israel's; the sweep of the conquest — a great land won from two mighty kings — set the seal on God's power and prepared the way for the crossing of Jordan, the first inheritance secured before the people entered Canaan itself.

So the witnesses make the verse the whole land taken: The witnesses note the the two Amorite kings: Sihon and Og. The witnesses note the from Arnon to Hermon: the whole east-Jordan land. The witnesses note the on this side Jordan: as Moses wrote it then. The witnesses note the the first inheritance: secured before the crossing. The expositors observe the the two Amorite kings: Sihon and Og. The expositors observe the from Arnon to Hermon: the whole east-Jordan land. The expositors observe the on this side Jordan: as Moses wrote it then. The expositors observe the the first inheritance: secured before the crossing. The believer is taught to mark the the two Amorite kings, the from Arnon to Hermon, the on this side Jordan, the the first inheritance, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Israel took the whole land of the two Amorite kings from Arnon to Hermon — the first inheritance secured by God's two victories before the crossing of Jordan.
9:
"(Which Hermon the Sidonians call Sirion; and the Amorites call it Shenir;)" The witnesses note Mount Hermon known by various names among the neighbouring peoples. The witnesses note that Gill notes Sirion was the name the Sidonians gave it, well known to Tyre and Sidon, whence snow was brought in summer. The witnesses note that Clarke takes the verse to be an explanatory note on the various names. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Hermon — the great snow-capped mountain; the called Sirion — by the Sidonians; the called Shenir — by the Amorites; the known to all around — a landmark to the surrounding peoples; the the northern bound — marking the reach of Israel's new possession.

The witnesses agree the noting of Hermon's several names fixes the northern bound of the conquest with care: the same great snow-capped mountain was called Sirion by the Sidonians and Shenir by the Amorites, a landmark known to all the surrounding peoples; the careful geographical note, marking the famed mountain that bounded the land, lends the record exactness and shows the reach of Israel's new possession to the very edge of the known northern country.

So the witnesses make the verse Hermon's names: The witnesses note the Hermon: the great snow-capped mountain. The witnesses note the called Sirion: by the Sidonians. The witnesses note the called Shenir: by the Amorites. The witnesses note the known to all around: a landmark to the surrounding peoples. The witnesses note the the northern bound: marking the reach of Israel's new possession. The expositors observe the Hermon: the great snow-capped mountain. The expositors observe the called Sirion: by the Sidonians. The expositors observe the called Shenir: by the Amorites. The expositors observe the known to all around: a landmark to the surrounding peoples. The expositors observe the the northern bound: marking the reach of Israel's new possession. The believer is taught to mark the Hermon, the called Sirion, the called Shenir, the known to all around, the the northern bound, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Hermon was called Sirion and Shenir by the neighbouring peoples — the famed mountain marking the northern bound of Israel's new possession, the record fixed with care.
10:
"All the cities of the plain, and all Gilead, and all Bashan, unto Salchah and Edrei, cities of the kingdom of Og in Bashan." The witnesses note the full extent of Og's kingdom now possessed — the plain, Gilead, and all Bashan to Salchah and Edrei. The witnesses note that Gill notes there was a plain by Medeba, and Heshbon and her cities were in a plain, given to Reuben. The witnesses note that Ellicott identifies Salchah with the present large town Salkhad, east of Bashan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the cities of the plain — and all Gilead; the all Bashan — to its farthest towns; the Salchah and Edrei — the bounds of Og's kingdom; the the whole realm — the giant king's land secured entire.

The witnesses agree the listing of the cities marks the breadth of the territory God had given: the whole kingdom of Og — the cities of the plain, all Gilead, all Bashan to its farthest towns — was now Israel's by conquest; the careful naming of the bounds, from the plain to Salchah and Edrei, shows the completeness of the gift, a great and fertile land secured entire, the LORD having delivered up the whole realm of the giant king.

So the witnesses make the verse the kingdom possessed: The witnesses note the the cities of the plain: and all Gilead. The witnesses note the all Bashan: to its farthest towns. The witnesses note the Salchah and Edrei: the bounds of Og's kingdom. The witnesses note the the whole realm: the giant king's land secured entire. The expositors observe the the cities of the plain: and all Gilead. The expositors observe the all Bashan: to its farthest towns. The expositors observe the Salchah and Edrei: the bounds of Og's kingdom. The expositors observe the the whole realm: the giant king's land secured entire. The believer is taught to mark the the cities of the plain, the all Bashan, the Salchah and Edrei, the the whole realm, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that the whole kingdom of Og — the plain, Gilead, and all Bashan to Salchah and Edrei — was possessed by Israel, the giant king's realm secured entire by God's gift.
11:
"For only Og king of Bashan remained of the remnant of giants; behold, his bedstead was a bedstead of iron... nine cubits was the length thereof, and four cubits the breadth of it..." The witnesses note Og the last of the giants, his huge iron bedstead a memorial of his great stature. The witnesses note that Gill notes Og was the only one left of the race of giants, his iron bedstead nine cubits long preserved as a memorial of his bulk and strength. The witnesses note that the dimensions witness his gigantic size. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the remnant of giants — og the last of the race; the a bedstead of iron — nine cubits long, a wonder; the his great stature — witnessed by its size; the the mightiest overthrown — the dread giant falling before God.

The witnesses agree the note of Og's iron bedstead, nine cubits long, magnifies the greatness of the victory God gave: the last of the giants, a man of such bulk that his very bed was a wonder, was overthrown by Israel; the preserving of so striking a memorial fixes the lesson — that the mightiest of men, the very remnant of the dread giants, fell before the God who fought for his people, so that no greatness of the foe should ever make the faithful afraid.

So the witnesses make the verse the last of the giants: The witnesses note the the remnant of giants: Og the last of the race. The witnesses note the a bedstead of iron: nine cubits long, a wonder. The witnesses note the his great stature: witnessed by its size. The witnesses note the the mightiest overthrown: the dread giant falling before God. The expositors observe the the remnant of giants: Og the last of the race. The expositors observe the a bedstead of iron: nine cubits long, a wonder. The expositors observe the his great stature: witnessed by its size. The expositors observe the the mightiest overthrown: the dread giant falling before God. The believer is taught to mark the the remnant of giants, the a bedstead of iron, the his great stature, the the mightiest overthrown, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Og, the last of the giants, with his iron bedstead nine cubits long, was overthrown — the mightiest of men falling before the God who fights for his people, that the faithful never fear the greatness of the foe.
12:
"And this land, which we possessed at that time, from Aroer, which is by the river Arnon, and half mount Gilead, and the cities thereof, gave I unto the Reubenites and to the Gadites." The witnesses note the conquered land from Aroer and half Gilead given to Reuben and Gad. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land was possessed and still remained in their hand. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the review of the distribution — from Aroer by Arnon and half Gilead given to the Reubenites and Gadites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the this land possessed — the conquered territory; the from Aroer — and half mount Gilead; the to Reuben and Gad — the tribes who sought it; the the inheritance divided — god's gift orderly parcelled out.

The witnesses agree the distribution of the conquered land begins the settling of the inheritance: the southern portion, from Aroer and half Gilead, was given to Reuben and Gad, the tribes who had sought it; the parcelling out of the land God had given shows the orderly fulfilment of his gift, the territory won by his power now divided among his people, each tribe receiving its appointed portion of the inheritance.

So the witnesses make the verse the land distributed: The witnesses note the this land possessed: the conquered territory. The witnesses note the from Aroer: and half mount Gilead. The witnesses note the to Reuben and Gad: the tribes who sought it. The witnesses note the the inheritance divided: God's gift orderly parcelled out. The expositors observe the this land possessed: the conquered territory. The expositors observe the from Aroer: and half mount Gilead. The expositors observe the to Reuben and Gad: the tribes who sought it. The expositors observe the the inheritance divided: God's gift orderly parcelled out. The believer is taught to mark the this land possessed, the from Aroer, the to Reuben and Gad, the the inheritance divided, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that the land from Aroer and half Gilead was given to Reuben and Gad — the orderly settling of the inheritance, the territory God won now divided among his people.
13:
"And the rest of Gilead, and all Bashan, being the kingdom of Og, gave I unto the half tribe of Manasseh; all the region of Argob, with all Bashan, which was called the land of giants." The witnesses note the rest of Gilead and all Bashan — the kingdom of Og — given to half Manasseh. The witnesses note that Gill notes the other half of the mount with its cities was given to half Manasseh. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set all Bashan, the land of giants, given to the half tribe. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the rest of Gilead — and all Bashan; the the kingdom of Og — the giant king's realm; the to half Manasseh — the northern inheritance; the the land of giants — become the home of God's people.

The witnesses agree the northern portion, the very kingdom of the giant Og, was given to half Manasseh: the land "called the land of giants" became the inheritance of God's people, the realm of the dread Rephaim now possessed by Israel; the giving of the giants' own country to the half tribe is a striking token that God overturns the strongholds of the mighty and plants his people in their place, the land of giants become the home of the faithful.

So the witnesses make the verse Bashan to Manasseh: The witnesses note the the rest of Gilead: and all Bashan. The witnesses note the the kingdom of Og: the giant king's realm. The witnesses note the to half Manasseh: the northern inheritance. The witnesses note the the land of giants: become the home of God's people. The expositors observe the the rest of Gilead: and all Bashan. The expositors observe the the kingdom of Og: the giant king's realm. The expositors observe the to half Manasseh: the northern inheritance. The expositors observe the the land of giants: become the home of God's people. The believer is taught to mark the the rest of Gilead, the the kingdom of Og, the to half Manasseh, the the land of giants, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that the rest of Gilead and all Bashan, the kingdom of Og, was given to half Manasseh — the very land of giants become the inheritance of God's people, the strongholds of the mighty overturned.
14:
"Jair the son of Manasseh took all the country of Argob unto the coasts of Geshuri and Maachathi; and called them after his own name, Bashan-havoth-jair, unto this day." The witnesses note Jair taking the country of Argob and naming it after himself, Bashan-havoth-jair. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jair took all the country of Argob, or Trachonitis, the small towns of Gilead. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the words "unto this day" seem to point to a later hand, as of Joshua, describing the completion. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took all Argob — the country of Trachonitis; the called after his name — bashan-havoth-jair; the unto this day — a lasting memorial of his valour; the the land settled — held by Manasseh, won by Jair.

The witnesses agree the conquest and naming of Argob by Jair marks the settling of the land by the energy of its conquerors: he took the whole region and called it by his own name, a memorial "unto this day" of his valour; the note that this phrase may come from a later hand simply confirms the record's standing as a witness to the lasting possession — the towns of Bashan held by Manasseh and bearing the name of the man who won them, the inheritance secured and remembered.

So the witnesses make the verse Jair's conquest: The witnesses note the took all Argob: the country of Trachonitis. The witnesses note the called after his name: Bashan-havoth-jair. The witnesses note the unto this day: a lasting memorial of his valour. The witnesses note the the land settled: held by Manasseh, won by Jair. The expositors observe the took all Argob: the country of Trachonitis. The expositors observe the called after his name: Bashan-havoth-jair. The expositors observe the unto this day: a lasting memorial of his valour. The expositors observe the the land settled: held by Manasseh, won by Jair. The believer is taught to mark the took all Argob, the called after his name, the unto this day, the the land settled, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Jair took Argob and named it after himself, Bashan-havoth-jair — the land settled by the valour of its conqueror, a lasting memorial of the inheritance secured.
15:
"And I gave Gilead unto Machir." The witnesses note Gilead given to Machir — that is, to his posterity, the Machirites. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was not to Machir personally, who cannot be thought living then, but to his posterity, the Machirites. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Machir received Gilead (Num. 32:40). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I gave Gilead — the northern portion; the unto Machir — that is, his posterity; the not the dead father — machir himself long gone; the the promise to the sons — the inheritance passing to the new generation.

The witnesses agree the grant of Gilead to "Machir" means to his descendants, the new generation entering the inheritance: Machir himself, of the old generation, was surely dead, so the land passed to his children who had won it; the giving of the portion to the sons rather than the long-dead father sounds again the theme of these chapters — that the promise is fulfilled to the children, the inheritance passing to the generation that goes in to possess it.

So the witnesses make the verse Gilead to Machir: The witnesses note the I gave Gilead: the northern portion. The witnesses note the unto Machir: that is, his posterity. The witnesses note the not the dead father: Machir himself long gone. The witnesses note the the promise to the sons: the inheritance passing to the new generation. The expositors observe the I gave Gilead: the northern portion. The expositors observe the unto Machir: that is, his posterity. The expositors observe the not the dead father: Machir himself long gone. The expositors observe the the promise to the sons: the inheritance passing to the new generation. The believer is taught to mark the I gave Gilead, the unto Machir, the not the dead father, the the promise to the sons, and to trust the God who fights for his people. The verse thus records that Gilead was given to Machir — to his posterity who won it, the inheritance passing to the children, the promise fulfilled to the generation that goes in to possess it.
16:
"And unto the Reubenites and unto the Gadites I gave from Gilead even unto the river Arnon half the valley, and the border even unto the river Jabbok, which is the border of the children of Ammon;" The witnesses note the bounds of the land given to Reuben and Gad, from Gilead to Arnon and to the Jabbok. The witnesses note that Gill notes the gift to the tribes of Reuben and Gad. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the bounds — from Gilead to Arnon, and to the Jabbok, the border of Ammon. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Gilead to Arnon — the southern reach; the to the river Jabbok — the border of Ammon; the to Reuben and Gad — their defined portion; the order and justice — ammon's reserved border honoured.

The witnesses agree the careful marking of the borders shows the orderly settling of the inheritance: the land given to Reuben and Gad is bounded exactly, from Gilead down to Arnon and over to the Jabbok, which marked Ammon's frontier; the precise fixing of the limits — respecting even the border of Ammon, which God had reserved — shows that the inheritance was parcelled with justice and order, each tribe's portion defined and the bounds God had set for others honoured.

So the witnesses make the verse the bounds set: The witnesses note the from Gilead to Arnon: the southern reach. The witnesses note the to the river Jabbok: the border of Ammon. The witnesses note the to Reuben and Gad: their defined portion. The witnesses note the order and justice: Ammon's reserved border honoured. The expositors observe the from Gilead to Arnon: the southern reach. The expositors observe the to the river Jabbok: the border of Ammon. The expositors observe the to Reuben and Gad: their defined portion. The expositors observe the order and justice: Ammon's reserved border honoured. The believer is taught to mark the from Gilead to Arnon, the to the river Jabbok, the to Reuben and Gad, the order and justice, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that the land to Reuben and Gad was bounded from Gilead to Arnon and to the Jabbok — the inheritance parcelled with order and justice, the bounds God set for Ammon honoured.
17:
"The plain also, and Jordan, and the coast thereof, from Chinnereth even unto the sea of the plain, even the salt sea, under Ashdoth-pisgah eastward." The witnesses note the plain and the Jordan valley, from Chinnereth to the Salt Sea, included in the portion. The witnesses note that Gill notes the plain by Jordan, the plains of Moab on its side, together with the river. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the Salt Sea was, before the dreadful conflagration, a goodly plain. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the plain and Jordan — the river valley; the from Chinnereth — to the Salt Sea; the the eastern portion — within the tribes' bounds; the once a goodly plain — the Salt Sea marking the judgment on Sodom.

The witnesses agree the bounds reach to the great landmarks of the Jordan valley: the plain, the river, from the Sea of Chinnereth (Galilee) to the Salt Sea, were all within the portion of the eastern tribes; Wesley's note that the Salt Sea was once a goodly plain recalls the judgment on Sodom that made it so — a quiet reminder, even in the marking of borders, of the God who both gives good lands to his people and turns the fruitful into a waste where sin abounds.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jordan valley: The witnesses note the the plain and Jordan: the river valley. The witnesses note the from Chinnereth: to the Salt Sea. The witnesses note the the eastern portion: within the tribes' bounds. The witnesses note the once a goodly plain: the Salt Sea marking the judgment on Sodom. The expositors observe the the plain and Jordan: the river valley. The expositors observe the from Chinnereth: to the Salt Sea. The expositors observe the the eastern portion: within the tribes' bounds. The expositors observe the once a goodly plain: the Salt Sea marking the judgment on Sodom. The believer is taught to mark the the plain and Jordan, the from Chinnereth, the the eastern portion, the once a goodly plain, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that the plain and Jordan from Chinnereth to the Salt Sea fell within the portion — the bounds marked to the great landmarks, the Salt Sea recalling the God who gives good lands and judges sin.
18:
"And I commanded you at that time, saying, The LORD your God hath given you this land to possess it: ye shall pass over armed before your brethren the children of Israel, all that are meet for the war." The witnesses note the charge to the eastern tribes — though given their land, to go armed before their brethren to the war. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was spoken not to all Israel but to Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh, whom alone it concerned. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the agreement that the two tribes and a half should not enjoy their possession unless they helped their brethren win theirs. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the land given them — their portion already secured; the pass over armed — to the war beyond Jordan; the before your brethren — in the forefront of the host; the the bond of brotherhood — the settled not living to themselves (Gal. 6:2).

The reformed reader (Calvin) marks the binding condition of brotherhood: the eastern tribes, though already given their land, were not to enjoy it at ease while their brethren still fought, but must go armed before them till all had their inheritance; the rule teaches that the settled may not forget the unsettled, nor the blessed live to themselves, but bear their part in the common warfare — a pattern of the mutual love that seeks not its own, but labours for the good of all the household of faith (cf. Gal. 6:2).

So the witnesses make the verse armed before their brethren: The witnesses note the the land given them: their portion already secured. The witnesses note the pass over armed: to the war beyond Jordan. The witnesses note the before your brethren: in the forefront of the host. The witnesses note the the bond of brotherhood: the settled not living to themselves (Gal. 6:2). The expositors observe the the land given them: their portion already secured. The expositors observe the pass over armed: to the war beyond Jordan. The expositors observe the before your brethren: in the forefront of the host. The expositors observe the the bond of brotherhood: the settled not living to themselves (Gal. 6:2). The believer is taught to mark the the land given them, the pass over armed, the before your brethren, the the bond of brotherhood, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes, though given their land, were charged to go armed before their brethren — the settled not living to themselves but bearing their part in the common warfare (Gal. 6:2).
19:
"But your wives, and your little ones, and your cattle, (for I know that ye have much cattle,) shall abide in your cities which I have given you;" The witnesses note that the families and cattle of the eastern tribes were to remain in their cities while the men went to war. The witnesses note that Gill notes the wives, little ones, and cattle were to be left behind. The witnesses note that they would abide in the cities God had given them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wives and little ones — left in the cities; the and your cattle — their much substance; the abide in your cities — safe in the new land; the the cost borne — the dear left while the men went to war.

The witnesses agree the provision both secured the families and made plain the cost the men would bear: their wives, children, and much cattle were to stay safe in the new cities while the warriors crossed Jordan to fight; the leaving of all that was dear behind, committed to God's keeping, shows the willingness of the eastern tribes to put their brethren's need before their own ease, going to a long war while their families dwelt in the rest they themselves deferred.

So the witnesses make the verse the families remain: The witnesses note the wives and little ones: left in the cities. The witnesses note the and your cattle: their much substance. The witnesses note the abide in your cities: safe in the new land. The witnesses note the the cost borne: the dear left while the men went to war. The expositors observe the wives and little ones: left in the cities. The expositors observe the and your cattle: their much substance. The expositors observe the abide in your cities: safe in the new land. The expositors observe the the cost borne: the dear left while the men went to war. The believer is taught to mark the wives and little ones, the and your cattle, the abide in your cities, the the cost borne, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that the families and cattle of the eastern tribes were to remain while the men went to war — the dear left in God's keeping, their brethren's need put before their own ease.
20:
"Until the LORD have given rest unto your brethren, as well as unto you, and until they also possess the land which the LORD your God hath given them beyond Jordan: and then shall ye return every man unto his possession..." The witnesses note the term of the service — until the LORD had given rest to all the tribes in their land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land of Canaan is called a rest, typical of the rest that remains for the people of God. The witnesses note that then each might return to his own possession. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rest unto your brethren — the land a rest for all; the as well as unto you — none entering ease alone; the then shall ye return — each to his possession; the the rest that remains — canaan a type of the eternal rest (Heb. 4:9).

The witnesses agree the word "rest" lifts the whole matter toward its gospel meaning: the eastern tribes were to labour until their brethren had "rest" in the land, Canaan itself being a type of the rest that remains for the people of God; the deferring of their own ease until all had entered the rest pictures the believer's warfare before the eternal rest, and the truth that there "remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God" (Heb. 4:9), into which all the faithful shall at last together enter.

So the witnesses make the verse until rest is given: The witnesses note the rest unto your brethren: the land a rest for all. The witnesses note the as well as unto you: none entering ease alone. The witnesses note the then shall ye return: each to his possession. The witnesses note the the rest that remains: Canaan a type of the eternal rest (Heb. 4:9). The expositors observe the rest unto your brethren: the land a rest for all. The expositors observe the as well as unto you: none entering ease alone. The expositors observe the then shall ye return: each to his possession. The expositors observe the the rest that remains: Canaan a type of the eternal rest (Heb. 4:9). The believer is taught to mark the rest unto your brethren, the as well as unto you, the then shall ye return, the the rest that remains, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes were to labour until the LORD gave rest to all — Canaan a type of the rest that remains for the people of God, into which the faithful shall together enter (Heb. 4:9).
21:
"And I commanded Joshua at that time, saying, Thine eyes have seen all that the LORD your God hath done unto these two kings: so shall the LORD do unto all the kingdoms whither thou passest." The witnesses note Moses charging Joshua, pointing to the two kings' defeat as a pledge of all the conquests to come. The witnesses note that Gill notes this charge was given after the conquest of the two kings, and after Moses knew he should die and Joshua succeed him. The witnesses note that Henry marks the encouragement Moses gave to Joshua, his successor. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thine eyes have seen — the defeat of the two kings; the so shall the LORD do — to all the kingdoms; the the past a pledge — the warrant of future victory; the remember God's works — the ground of faith for what remains (1 Sam. 17:37).

The witnesses agree Moses grounded Joshua's courage in what his own eyes had seen: the LORD who had overthrown Sihon and Og would do the same to all the kingdoms of Canaan, the past victory a sure pledge of those to come; the encouragement teaches that God's former works are the warrant of faith for future trials, the believer bidden to remember what God has already done as the ground of confidence that he will finish what he has begun (cf. 1 Sam. 17:37).

So the witnesses make the verse the pledge to Joshua: The witnesses note the thine eyes have seen: the defeat of the two kings. The witnesses note the so shall the LORD do: to all the kingdoms. The witnesses note the the past a pledge: the warrant of future victory. The witnesses note the remember God's works: the ground of faith for what remains (1 Sam. 17:37). The expositors observe the thine eyes have seen: the defeat of the two kings. The expositors observe the so shall the LORD do: to all the kingdoms. The expositors observe the the past a pledge: the warrant of future victory. The expositors observe the remember God's works: the ground of faith for what remains (1 Sam. 17:37). The believer is taught to mark the thine eyes have seen, the so shall the LORD do, the the past a pledge, the remember God's works, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that Moses charged Joshua, the defeat of the two kings a pledge of all to come — God's former works the warrant of faith, the ground of confidence that he will finish what he began (1 Sam. 17:37).
22:
"Ye shall not fear them: for the LORD your God he shall fight for you." The witnesses note the ground of courage — they need not fear, for the LORD himself would fight for them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were not to fear the numbers, strength, courage, or gigantic stature of the inhabitants, nor their walled cities. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the reason — Jehovah himself would fight for them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye shall not fear — neither giants nor walls; the the LORD your God — his own presence in the battle; the he shall fight for you — the warfare his; the fear cast out — if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31).

The witnesses agree this verse is the sum and ground of all the encouragement: Israel need fear neither giants nor walls nor numbers, "for the LORD your God he shall fight for you"; the promise of God's own presence in the battle is the believer's answer to every terror, that the warfare is the LORD's and the victory sure — "if God be for us, who can be against us?" (Rom. 8:31) — fear cast out by the assurance that he fights for his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD shall fight: The witnesses note the ye shall not fear: neither giants nor walls. The witnesses note the the LORD your God: his own presence in the battle. The witnesses note the he shall fight for you: the warfare his. The witnesses note the fear cast out: if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31). The expositors observe the ye shall not fear: neither giants nor walls. The expositors observe the the LORD your God: his own presence in the battle. The expositors observe the he shall fight for you: the warfare his. The expositors observe the fear cast out: if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31). The believer is taught to mark the ye shall not fear, the the LORD your God, the he shall fight for you, the fear cast out, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that Israel need not fear, for the LORD himself would fight for them — the sum of all encouragement, fear cast out by the assurance that God fights for his people (Rom. 8:31).
23:
"And I besought the LORD at that time, saying," The witnesses note Moses earnestly beseeching the LORD, longing to enter the good land. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses, when told he should die and Joshua succeed him, and seeing the conquest of the two kings as the earnest of the promise, was desirous to live to see the work completed, and sought the Lord by prayer. The witnesses note that he besought the LORD at that time. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I besought the LORD — earnest prayer; the at that time — seeing the first victories; the longing to enter — to see the work completed; the desire poured out — leaving the answer in God's hands.

The witnesses agree the verse opens a tender passage of Moses' heart: seeing the first victories as the pledge of the whole promise, the aged servant longed to enter the land himself and besought the LORD for it; the earnestness of his prayer — the man of God pleading to see the good land — shows both the goodness of the inheritance that drew such longing, and the humility of one who, knowing the sentence on him, yet poured out his desire to God, leaving the answer in his hands.

So the witnesses make the verse Moses besought: The witnesses note the I besought the LORD: earnest prayer. The witnesses note the at that time: seeing the first victories. The witnesses note the longing to enter: to see the work completed. The witnesses note the desire poured out: leaving the answer in God's hands. The expositors observe the I besought the LORD: earnest prayer. The expositors observe the at that time: seeing the first victories. The expositors observe the longing to enter: to see the work completed. The expositors observe the desire poured out: leaving the answer in God's hands. The believer is taught to mark the I besought the LORD, the at that time, the longing to enter, the desire poured out, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that Moses besought the LORD, longing to enter the land — the aged servant pouring out his desire in earnest prayer, leaving the answer in God's hands.
24:
"O Lord GOD, thou hast begun to shew thy servant thy greatness, and thy mighty hand: for what God is there in heaven or in earth, that can do according to thy works, and according to thy might?" The witnesses note Moses praising God's greatness and incomparable might, seen in the recent victories. The witnesses note that Gill notes God had begun to show his greatness in subduing the two kings; Moses had seen his power in Egypt, at the Red sea, and in the wilderness, but this was the beginning of his power against the Canaanites. The witnesses note that none in heaven or earth can do according to his works. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thy greatness and might — seen in the victories; the thou hast begun — the conquest's first works; the what God is like thee — none in heaven or earth; the worship in prayer — moses magnifying the peerless LORD (Exod. 15:11).

The witnesses agree Moses' prayer rises into adoration of the incomparable God: the subduing of the two kings was but the "beginning" of God's mighty works in the conquest, and the sight of it drew from Moses the confession that no god in heaven or earth can match his works and might; the worship woven into his petition shows the right temper of prayer, that even in pleading his own desire Moses magnified the LORD, owning him peerless — "who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods?" (Exod. 15:11).

So the witnesses make the verse the incomparable God: The witnesses note the thy greatness and might: seen in the victories. The witnesses note the thou hast begun: the conquest's first works. The witnesses note the what God is like thee: none in heaven or earth. The witnesses note the worship in prayer: Moses magnifying the peerless LORD (Exod. 15:11). The expositors observe the thy greatness and might: seen in the victories. The expositors observe the thou hast begun: the conquest's first works. The expositors observe the what God is like thee: none in heaven or earth. The expositors observe the worship in prayer: Moses magnifying the peerless LORD (Exod. 15:11). The believer is taught to mark the thy greatness and might, the thou hast begun, the what God is like thee, the worship in prayer, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that Moses praised God's incomparable greatness and might — worship woven into his prayer, owning the LORD peerless, who is like unto thee among the gods (Exod. 15:11).
25:
"I pray thee, let me go over, and see the good land that is beyond Jordan, that goodly mountain, and Lebanon." The witnesses note the heart of Moses' prayer — to cross over and see the good land of promise. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses was desirous of going over Jordan to see the land flowing with milk and honey, a most excellent land. The witnesses note that Wesley notes he supposed God's threatening might admit of being reversed by prayer. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let me go over — to cross Jordan; the see the good land — the inheritance of promise; the that goodly mountain — and Lebanon; the the boldness of faith — yet God's decree to stand.

The witnesses agree the petition lays bare the longing of a heart set on the promised inheritance: Moses prayed to cross and see "the good land," the goodly mountain and Lebanon, the land he had led toward for forty years; the desire was holy, a longing for the place of God's blessing, and even his supposing the sentence might be reversed shows the boldness of faith in prayer — yet the answer would teach that God's wise and righteous decrees stand, and that some longings, however good, are not granted as we ask.

So the witnesses make the verse let me go over: The witnesses note the let me go over: to cross Jordan. The witnesses note the see the good land: the inheritance of promise. The witnesses note the that goodly mountain: and Lebanon. The witnesses note the the boldness of faith: yet God's decree to stand. The expositors observe the let me go over: to cross Jordan. The expositors observe the see the good land: the inheritance of promise. The expositors observe the that goodly mountain: and Lebanon. The expositors observe the the boldness of faith: yet God's decree to stand. The believer is taught to mark the let me go over, the see the good land, the that goodly mountain, the the boldness of faith, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that Moses prayed to cross and see the good land — a holy longing for the place of blessing, the boldness of faith in prayer, yet God's righteous decree to stand unmoved.
26:
"But the LORD was wroth with me for your sakes, and would not hear me: and the LORD said unto me, Let it suffice thee; speak no more unto me of this matter." The witnesses note God's refusal — wroth for Israel's sake, he bade Moses speak no more of it. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD was wroth not for this prayer but on account of Moses and Aaron not sanctifying him at the waters of Meribah, and the unadvised words that dropped through Israel's provocation. The witnesses note that he was bidden to speak no more of the matter. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wroth for your sakes — the fault at Meribah recalled; the would not hear me — the prayer denied; the let it suffice thee — the matter closed; the submission learned — god may say no to a holy plea.

The witnesses agree the refusal was firm yet not unkind, and rooted in the old fault at Meribah: God would not grant Moses' prayer to enter, the sentence for his rash words standing, and he bade him "speak no more of this matter"; the denial teaches that God may say no even to a faithful servant's earnest and holy prayer, that his righteous decrees are not always bent to our desires, and that the believer must learn, with Moses, to let God's "let it suffice thee" silence even the dearest plea — submitting to the wisdom that withholds.

So the witnesses make the verse God's refusal: The witnesses note the wroth for your sakes: the fault at Meribah recalled. The witnesses note the would not hear me: the prayer denied. The witnesses note the let it suffice thee: the matter closed. The witnesses note the submission learned: God may say no to a holy plea. The expositors observe the wroth for your sakes: the fault at Meribah recalled. The expositors observe the would not hear me: the prayer denied. The expositors observe the let it suffice thee: the matter closed. The expositors observe the submission learned: God may say no to a holy plea. The believer is taught to mark the wroth for your sakes, the would not hear me, the let it suffice thee, the submission learned, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that God, wroth for the fault at Meribah, denied Moses' prayer and bade him speak no more — God saying no even to a faithful servant's holy plea, the believer learning to submit to the wisdom that withholds.
27:
"Get thee up into the top of Pisgah, and lift up thine eyes westward, and northward, and southward, and eastward, and behold it with thine eyes: for thou shalt not go over this Jordan." The witnesses note God granting Moses to see the land from Pisgah, though not to enter it. The witnesses note that Gill notes Pisgah was the highest eminence of Mount Nebo, a proper place to take a prospect. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him bidden to behold the land on every side, but not to go over Jordan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the get thee up Pisgah — the high place of prospect; the behold it with thine eyes — the land on every side; the not go over Jordan — the entrance still denied; the judgment and kindness — the law seeing the rest, not bringing in (Heb. 4:8).

The witnesses agree God tempered his refusal with a gracious sight: Moses might not enter, yet was bidden to climb Pisgah and behold the land on every side with his own eyes; the LORD, denying the greater request, granted a real and tender mercy, letting his servant see the inheritance he had longed for though he should not tread it — a mingling of judgment and kindness, and a figure of how the law (in Moses) may bring the people to the very border of the rest, and see it, yet not itself bring them in (cf. Heb. 4:8).

So the witnesses make the verse behold it from Pisgah: The witnesses note the get thee up Pisgah: the high place of prospect. The witnesses note the behold it with thine eyes: the land on every side. The witnesses note the not go over Jordan: the entrance still denied. The witnesses note the judgment and kindness: the law seeing the rest, not bringing in (Heb. 4:8). The expositors observe the get thee up Pisgah: the high place of prospect. The expositors observe the behold it with thine eyes: the land on every side. The expositors observe the not go over Jordan: the entrance still denied. The expositors observe the judgment and kindness: the law seeing the rest, not bringing in (Heb. 4:8). The believer is taught to mark the get thee up Pisgah, the behold it with thine eyes, the not go over Jordan, the judgment and kindness, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that God let Moses behold the land from Pisgah though not enter it — judgment tempered with mercy, a figure of how the law brings to the border of the rest and sees it, yet brings not in (Heb. 4:8).
28:
"But charge Joshua, and encourage him, and strengthen him: for he shall go over before this people, and he shall cause them to inherit the land which thou shalt see." The witnesses note Moses bidden to charge and strengthen Joshua, who should bring the people into the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses was to charge Joshua to take care of the people, encourage him against fear of his enemies, and strengthen him with the promises of God's presence and help. The witnesses note that Joshua should go over and cause Israel to inherit. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the charge and strengthen him — preparing the successor; the he shall go over — before the people; the cause them to inherit — bringing them into the land; the the true Joshua — bringing in what Moses could not.

The witnesses agree the passing of the charge to Joshua marks the gracious continuance of God's work beyond Moses: though Moses might not enter, the people would not be left without a leader, for Joshua should go before them and bring them into the inheritance; the bidding to "encourage and strengthen" his successor shows the duty of preparing those who come after, and the comfort that God's purpose for his people does not fail with the passing of one servant — Joshua (whose name is Jesus) bringing in what Moses could not, the leader of the rest a figure of the true Joshua who brings his people home.

So the witnesses make the verse charge Joshua: The witnesses note the charge and strengthen him: preparing the successor. The witnesses note the he shall go over: before the people. The witnesses note the cause them to inherit: bringing them into the land. The witnesses note the the true Joshua: bringing in what Moses could not. The expositors observe the charge and strengthen him: preparing the successor. The expositors observe the he shall go over: before the people. The expositors observe the cause them to inherit: bringing them into the land. The expositors observe the the true Joshua: bringing in what Moses could not. The believer is taught to mark the charge and strengthen him, the he shall go over, the cause them to inherit, the the true Joshua, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that Moses was to charge and strengthen Joshua, who should bring Israel into the land — God's work continuing beyond Moses, Joshua a figure of the true Joshua who brings his people into the rest.
29:
"So we abode in the valley over against Beth-peor." The witnesses note Israel abiding in the valley over against Beth-peor, where Moses would be buried. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was in the plains of Moab, over against a temple of Baal-peor upon a mountain; this is the valley where Moses was buried. The witnesses note that they abode there. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we abode — israel's last encampment; the over against Beth-peor — near the temple of Baal-peor; the the place of Moses' grave — where he would die and be buried; the the stage set — the people poised by Jordan to hear the law.

The witnesses agree the closing verse fixes the place of Israel's last encampment before the crossing, and the place of Moses' grave: they abode in the valley over against Beth-peor, the very scene of the recent sin of Baal-peor and the place where Moses would die and be buried; the quiet note, naming the spot opposite the temple of the idol, sets the stage for the rest of Deuteronomy — the people poised by Jordan, hearing the law from Moses' lips in the last days of his life, the great servant near his end and the land near at hand.

So the witnesses make the verse the valley by Beth-peor: The witnesses note the we abode: Israel's last encampment. The witnesses note the over against Beth-peor: near the temple of Baal-peor. The witnesses note the the place of Moses' grave: where he would die and be buried. The witnesses note the the stage set: the people poised by Jordan to hear the law. The expositors observe the we abode: Israel's last encampment. The expositors observe the over against Beth-peor: near the temple of Baal-peor. The expositors observe the the place of Moses' grave: where he would die and be buried. The expositors observe the the stage set: the people poised by Jordan to hear the law. The believer is taught to mark the we abode, the over against Beth-peor, the the place of Moses' grave, the the stage set, and to trust the God who gives his people rest. The verse thus records that Israel abode in the valley over against Beth-peor — the last encampment and the place of Moses' grave, the people poised by Jordan to hear the law in the great servant's last days.
